coredata.dc:title: Broadband nonlinear optical properties of red fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Wenxuan
He, Jun
Wei, Hao
Zhang, Chen
Zhu, Menglong
Xu, Defeng
Xiao, Si
He, Jiangling
Chen, Zhihui
Meng, Jianqiao
coredata.dc:description:
Carbon dots (CDs) provide ideal platforms for applications in bioimaging, sensing, and photocatalysis. Herein, a new type of red fluorescent carbon dot (RCD) is successfully synthesized, and its broadband nonlinear optical (NLO) properties are systematically characterized. The nonlinear absorption coefficient (
α
NL
) values of the RCDs at 580, 630, 650, 1064 nm are − 0.65, −3.09, −4.02, and 0.14 cm/GW, respectively. The nonlinear absorption transforms from saturable absorption to two-photon absorption in the near-infrared domain. We demonstrate the application of the RCDs for imaging Hela cells. Our results indicate that the synthesized RCDs with a broadband NLO response exhibit potential applications in saturable absorbers and deep-tissue imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nonlinear optics
Two-photon absorption
Fluorescence
Biological imaging

Potential applications of the RCDs in biological imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the synthesized RCDs. (a) Diagram illustrating the structure of the RCDs. (b) HRTEM image of the RCDs (Inset: lattice fringes derived from the region indicated by the yellow arrow). (c) Optical images of the RCDs taken under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. (d) Size distribution of the RCDs. (e) XPS survey spectrum of the RCDs. (f) FT-IR spectrum of the RCDs. (g) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the RCDs (Inset: magnified view of the absorption spectrum of the RCDs in the range from 450 to 700 nm). (h) PL spectrum of the RCDs. (i) TRPL curves of the RCDs under 405 nm excitation detected at different emission wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

OA Z-scans of the RCDs under (a) 580 nm, (b) 630 nm, (c) 650 nm, and (d) 1064 nm excitation for different excitation intensities. Inset: Normalized transmittance as a function of the excitation intensity.

(a) PL and 2PPL spectra of the RCDs under 405 and 800 nm excitation, respectively. (b) Comparison of the 2PA cross-section and absorption spectrum. (c) Schematic diagram of the SA, PL, and 2PPL processes.

(a) Optical microscopy image of the RCDs in cancer cells. PL intensity (b) and PL lifetime (c) imaging of RCDs-treated HeLa cells under 488 nm excitation. (d) 2PEF (λ ex: 820 nm, λ em: 520–700 nm) image. (e) Bright-field image. (f) Overlay confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) image of the HeLa cells treated with the RCDs for 24 h.

head.ce:title: Broadband nonlinear optical properties of red fluorescent carbon dots

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon dots (CDs) provide ideal platforms for applications in bioimaging, sensing, and photocatalysis. Herein, a new type of red fluorescent carbon dot (RCD) is successfully synthesized, and its broadband nonlinear optical (NLO) properties are systematically characterized. The nonlinear absorption coefficient (
α
NL
) values of the RCDs at 580, 630, 650, 1064 nm are − 0.65, −3.09, −4.02, and 0.14 cm/GW, respectively. The nonlinear absorption transforms from saturable absorption to two-photon absorption in the near-infrared domain. We demonstrate the application of the RCDs for imaging Hela cells. Our results indicate that the synthesized RCDs with a broadband NLO response exhibit potential applications in saturable absorbers and deep-tissue imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), which belong to the category of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted extensive attention due to their unique optical properties, electrical properties, and effective photoelectric conversion efficiency[1–5]. Furthermore, owing to their simple synthesis procedure and low cost, CDs are considered to be potential candidates for application in biosensing, fluorescent imaging, photocatalysis, and other fields [6–11]. CDs have been prepared using a wide variety of precursors (e.g., hydroxyl groups (−OH), carboxyl groups (−COOH), amino groups (−NH2), and double bonds (−C = C)) through several different synthesis methods, including hydrothermal reaction, laser ablation, pyrolytic process, and microwave-assisted method[12,13]. Despite the variety of preparation methods, CDs are generally fabricated using conjugated carbon cores and amorphous shells with a common sp 2 core domain, which can contain nitrogen and oxygen in the aromatic ring system and has a variety of functional groups on its surface, such as hydroxyl, amide, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups. These functional groups endow CDs with numerous beneficial properties[14,15].

The optical properties of CDs can be fine-tuned through the functional groups. Holá et al. reported that full-color CDs with a well-controllable fluorescence from the visible to the near-infrared (NIR) range can be prepared through the solvothermal decomposition of urea and citric acid in formamide[16]. Their results indicated that the presence of graphite nitrogen permits the realization of a fluorescence redshift in the CDs. Due to the superior tissue permeability of red light and the fact that, compared with other red-emission nanomaterials, e.g., quantum dots and organic dyes, red fluorescent carbon dots (RCDs) are always highly resistant to photobleaching and have strong absorption and biodegradability in the red/NIR region, RCDs have attracted continuous attention from the research community[17–20]. Multiphoton photoluminescence (MPPL) imaging, which exhibits a larger penetration depth and higher spatial resolution than photoluminescence (PL) imaging, shows potential applications in the bio-imaging and sensing fields, since the MPPL is mainly generated within the focal volume[21–26]. Alaghmandfard et al. used CDs as an upconversion PL probe to conduct NIR two-photon imaging. Their results confirmed that CDs can be used as fluorescent probes for biological imaging both in vivo and in vitro. Nasrin et al. injected CDs into the oral cancer cells as two-photon active photosensitizers. Under 780 nm laser excitation, the injection site could generate bright two-photon photoluminescence with a high signal-to-noise ratio. After nucleus-targeting photodynamic therapy, the DNA of cancer cells decomposed and was completely destroyed. These results reveal that MPPL imaging has potential applications in the bio-imaging and sensing fields as well as the treatment of cancer cells[27,28].

In addition to MPPL, the saturable absorption (SA) and multiphoton absorption (MPA) properties of CDs endow them with the possibility of being utilized in saturable absorbers and optical limiters[29,30]. The nonlinear optical (NLO) responses of CDs solutions and thin films were reported by Yin et al. and Nguyen et al., respectively. It was found that the nonlinear absorption (NLA) coefficient (
α
N L
) is closely related to the carbon nitride content in the CDs[31–33]. Thus far, few studies have paid particular attention to the SA properties of RCDs[34]. Therefore, it is still necessary and challenging to fabricate RCDs with superior SA and MPA properties.

Especially, the two-photon excitation (2PE) fluorescence has the advantages of deeper penetration depth, higher resolution and lower photodamage on the basis of traditional one-photon excitation (1PE) fluorescence[35,36]. Organic molecules generally have the two-photon absorption (2PA) cross section of only 1–300 GM (Göppert-Mayer), which is some weak for application of 2PE fluorescence imaging. Although 2PA cross section of semiconductor quantum dots can be over 10,000 GM, they are quite toxic because of heavy metal elements[37]. Therefore, organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots are not available for bio-imaging. The development of CDs with high 2PA cross section is a good approach to overcome these issues.

In this work, we prepared a new type of nitrogen-containing CDs mixture with a red fluorescence emission and found that its quantum yield (QY) can reach 30.4%. By establishing a systemic femtosecond laser pulse open-aperture (OA) Z-scan characterization system and studying the MPPL properties of the RCDs, we demonstrate the broadband NLO properties of the synthetized RCDs and prove their applicability in the biological imaging field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The RCDs were prepared according to the procedures illustrated in Scheme S1: 0.2 g of dopamine hydrochloride and 0.11 g of o-phenylenediamine were dispersed in 10 ml of deionized water. The mixture solution was subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ for 8 h. Based on the stoichiometric ratio of dopamine hydrochloride and o-phenylenediamine and the existing literature on RCDs materials, we constructed the structural diagram of the RCDs (Fig. 1 a)[38–40]. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) imaging shows that the obtained RCDs have a rather uniform particle size of 4–7 nm (Fig. 1b). The inset in Fig. 1b shows the as-prepared RCDs (indicate by the yellow arrow); the lattice fringes are clearly visible. The lattice parameter is around 0.19 nm, corresponding to the (102) lattice fringes of graphite, which indicates that the crystalline core of the RCDs possesses the graphite structure. The small particles (indicated by the red arrow) appear to be residues of RCDs located on different focal planes, as their lattice fringes are either absent or dimmed under high-magnification imaging. The as-prepared RCDs were dispersed in N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and displayed a clear purple color under daylight.

Under 365-nm ultraviolet (UV) light excitation, the solution generated a bright red emission, indicating the high QY of the RCDs (Fig. 1c). Approximately 100 RCDs in the photos were randomly selected as statistical samples (the statistical number for each RCD was acquired from several photos, which are not shown here). The average particle size of the RCDs was determined to be around 6 nm through Gaussian fitting (Fig. 1d).

To analyze the elemental composition of the RCDs, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 1e and f, the as-prepared RCDs mainly contain carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and chlorine, which is similar to the composition of other RCDs reported in the literature[41,42]. The peak at ∼ 3030 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of the C–H bond. The small peak at around 1700 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the –C = O bond. The characteristic peaks at around 1410 and 1600 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibration of the C–N bond and the existence of the C = C bond, respectively. The above results suggest that the surface of the CDs synthesized from 3-Hydroxytyramine hydrochloride and o-Phenylenediamine contains several functional groups, such as the C–H, –C = O, C = C, and C–N bonds.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum of the RCDs (2.66 × 10−6 mol/L) displays two main absorption bands: 260–350 nm in the UV region and 450–700 nm in the Vis region (Fig. 1g). The absorption band located at 450–700 nm is attributed to the high conjugation and high quality of the RCDs, which is in line with the absorption band of other previously reported CDs[43]. TheRCDs contain a large number of benzene rings with aromatic o-phenylenediamine and dopamine hydrochloride as precursors. The presence of benzene can increase the conjugation degree of the system and easily achieve the π–π* transition, resulting in a strong fluorescence[44]. The conjugation effect can increase the molar absorption coefficient of the CDs, which is beneficial for generating more excited-state molecules and thus enhancing the fluorescence. The absorption peak of the RCDs is the highest at 280 nm[45]. HCl can modify the PL properties of the CDs by changing the pH value of the reaction medium. A highly acidic environment leads to a redshift in the emission by increasing the carbonization degree and expanding the conjugated sp 2 structure of the CDs[46]. The existence of the C = O and C–N bonds in the RCDs induces the n–π* transition, which limits the improvement of the fluorescence properties of the biomass CDs to a certain extent. Therefore, the absorption peaks at 580 and 630 nm are weaker than that at 280 nm. The PL spectrum of the RCDs (Fig. 1h) exhibits multiple emissions, namely 598, 657, and 703 nm, owing to the π-domain controlled quantum size effect[47]. Using an integrating sphere, the PL QY of the RCDs was determined to be around 30.4%. The graphite cores of the RCDs determine their intrinsic radiation, while the attached chemical groups control the surface state[48,49]. The conjugate-domain band gaps can be approximated to be true intrinsic PL centers. The emissions generated by the radiative transitions of electrons between two energy levels do not undergo spin inversion[50,51].

The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) of the RCDs in DMF was also measured, as show in Fig. 1i. In the experiment, the excitation wavelength of the sample was set to 405 nm, and the emissions peaks were observed at 598, 657, and 703 nm. We fitted the obtained data, and the fitting results are shown in Table S1, where
A x
refers to the proportion of the fitted lifetime (
x
= 1, 2, 3), and
τ x
represents the corresponding fluorescence lifetime. The fitting results indicate that the RCDs undergo three decay processes. The average fluorescence lifetimes for each peak of the RCDs are 2.14 ns (598 nm), 1.42 ns (657 nm), and 1.36 ns (703 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NLO absorption properties of the RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the ultrafast NLO response of the RCDs, we performed conventional OA Z-scan experiments on an RCDs aqueous solution (1.60 × 10−6 mol/L) at wavelengths of 580, 630, 650, and 1064 nm. The pulse duration of the laser was 35 fs. The pulse width could become wider after the Z-scan system. The normalized transmittance as a function of the sample position on the Z-axis is presented in Fig. 2 . The typical “bell-shaped” OA Z-scan traces are observed at all wavelengths at different excitation intensities. The excitation intensity of the RCDs is proportional to the change in the peak signal. It can be concluded that the RCDs exhibit SA properties at 580, 630, and 650 nm owing to the bleaching effect under excitation. This is similar to the ground-state bleaching reported for other CDs[52]. The SA mechanism is shown in Fig. 3 c. When the excitation intensity is weak and the photon energy is greater than the band gap of the RCDs, the valence band electrons are excited and start to occupy the conduction band states. When the excitation light intensity is sufficiently strong, the available states in the conduction band are completely occupied by the photoexcited carriers. The optical bleaching effect (e.g., SA) occurs due to the Pauli blocking principle[53]. When changing the excitation wavelength to 1064 nm, the NLO response transforms to reverse SA. Tauc Plot was applied to fit the UV–Visible spectrum of RCDs (Fig. S5), its band gap was determined to be around ∼ 1.89 eV (656 nm). The corresponding photon energy of 2.13 (580 nm), 1.98 (630 nm) and 1.90 eV (650 nm) are larger than the band gap of RCDs. Especially, 580 and 630 nm are resonant wavelengths of RCDs as shown in UV–Visible spectrum, which could produce saturation behavior under the excitation of femtosecond laser. Whereas the photon energy of 1.17 eV (1064 nm) is not enough to stimulate linear absorption behavior. Two-photon absorption may occur when the photon energy is less than the band gap and larger than half the band gap. Therefore, the origin of RSA can be assigned to two-photon absorption.

The NLA coefficient of RCDs can be expressed as
α =
α 0
+
α
NL
I
, where
α 0
α 0
is the linear absorption coefficient,
α
NL
is the NLA coefficient, and
I
is the incident light intensity. The corresponding equation can be expressed as:
(1)
dI / d Z = -

I
I

When the incident light is a Gaussian beam, the normalized transmittance of the sample is defined as[54]:
(2)
T

=

, with

being the peak intensity of the incident light.

We assume that the effective thickness of the RCDs is

Where
L
is the sample thickness. All RCDs solutions were measured in a 1 mm cuvette.

The linear absorption coefficient (
α 0
) values of the RCDs at the four different wavelengths (580, 630, 650, and 1064 nm) were calculated to be 8.35, 7.70, 5.21, and 1.54 cm−1, respectively, while the NLA coefficient values were − 0.65, −3.09, −4.02, and 0.14 cm/GW, respectively, which are around two orders of magnitude higher than those of sulfur quantum dots (SQDs), black phosphorus (BP), MXene QDs, graphene, and other materials (Table S2)[55–58]. The
α
NL
value of the RCDs increases with the increase in wavelength. The normalized transmittance as a function of the excitation intensity and saturation intensity can be obtained by fitting the curves according to the following expression:
(4)
T = 1 -
A s
/
1 + I /
I
sat
-
A
ns

where
A s
is the modulation depth,
A
ns
is the contribution of the nonsaturable components,
I
sat
reflects the saturable intensity, and
I =
I 0
/
1 +
Z
2
/
Z
0
2
is the light intensity distribution on the Z-axis.
Z 0
= π
ω
0
2
/ λ
is the Rayleigh length of the Gaussian beam, and
ω 0
is the beam radius at the focal point. As shown in Fig. 2a–c (inset), the fitting curve is in good agreement with the experimental results. By fitting the experimental data, the saturation intensity and modulation depth can be obtained. The fitting results indicate the
I
sat
at 650 nm (18.85 GW/cm2) is relatively low compared with those at 580 nm (31.36 GW/cm2) and 630 nm (26.05 GW/cm2). The experimental results suggest that the RCDs are potential candidates for application in nonlinear photonic devices in the Vis and NIR bands, such as saturable absorbers.

2PA of the RCDs (2.66 × 10-6 mol/L) was systematically investigated under NIR (800–940 and 1134–1350 nm) excitation (Fig. S1). The two-photon PL (2PPL) spectrum (Fig. 3a, blue curve) is approximately identical to the single-photon PL spectrum under 405-nm excitation (Fig. 3a, pink curve), indicating a similar radiative channel. The small redshift (∼8 nm) of the 2PPL spectrum may result from repeated self-absorptions and emissions, which cause the RCDs to emit light with wavelength closer to the bottom of the band[59,60]. The slopes of the MPPL as a function of the laser power are around ∼ 2.0, indicating the occurrence of the 2PA process (Fig. S1). The slight deviations of the 2PA spectrum at some excitation wavelengths may be due to the occurrence of high-order absorption processes and the low acquisition efficiency of the MPA emission signal of the system. The higher-order upconverted NLO effect requires a longer-wavelength femtosecond laser illumination and is not discussed here due to the limitations of our experimental setup[60].

We obtained the 2PA cross section of the RCDs by comparing the integrated 2PPL intensities with those of Rhodamine 6G (R6G) (Fig. S2). The expression used is as follows (the details of the derivation are provided in the Supplementary Materials) [61]:
(5)
F
2
R 6 G
/
F
2
x
=
η
σ
2
R 6 G
·
ρ
R 6 G
·
I
00
2
R 6 G
/
η
σ
2

x
·
I
00
2
x

where
F
2
R 6 G
and
F
2
x
are the measured PL intensity of R6G and the RCDs, respectively,
η
is the PL QY,
σ 2
is the 2PA cross section,
ρ
is the sample molar concentration, and
I 00
is the peak intensity of the input laser pulse at the focal point.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential applications of the RCDs in biological imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the obtained NLO properties of the RCDs, we investigated the potential applications of the RCDs in the biological imaging field. Fig. 4 a shows an optical microscopy image of the RCDs within cancer cells. Fig. 4b and c show the fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) of the PL intensity and lifetime in RCDs-treated HeLa cells, respectively. FLIM is a highly sensitive method used to detect the microenvironment of fluorescent probes. Furthermore, it is independent of the concentration of fluorescent probes and excitation power. Confocal fluorescence imaging shows that RCD-labeled HeLa cells display a pronounced fluorescence, indicating the potential of RCDs in biological imaging.

Motivated by these findings, we conducted two-photon excited fluorescence (2PEF) in vitro imaging of these RCDs-labeled HeLa cells, as shown in Fig. 4d–f. A clear 2PEF signal is detected with an emission channel of 520–700 nm under 820 nm femtosecond laser excitation. Fig. S3 indicates the penetration depth along the Z-stacking scanning for the realization of the one-photon imaging of HeLa cancer cells under an excitation wavelength of 488 nm. The fluorescence signals of the RCDs were collected at a depth of 9.5 μm.

In summary, we developed a novel and simple method to synthesize liquid-state RCDs with QY of around 30.4% using dopamine hydrochloride, o-phenylenediamine, and HCl as the reactants. The average particle size of the RCDs is around 4–7 nm. We studied the visible SA and 2PA properties of the synthesized RCDs using ultrafast NLO spectroscopy. The NLA coefficient of the RCDs was calculated to be
α
NL
= − 0.65 cm/GW (580 nm), −3.09 cm/GW (630 nm), and − 4.02 cm/GW (650 nm).
α
NL
shifts toward a positive value when the incident light wavelength changes to 1064 nm (0.14 cm/GW), which indicates the occurrence of the 2PA effect. It is also worth noting that the RCDs exhibit a giant 2PA cross section (
σ 2
) of 30,160 GM (3.02 × 10−46cm4·s·photon−1) at 1256 nm. Thus, it is demonstrated that the RCDs are an efficient two-photon fluorescent probe for cellular and deep-tissue imaging. Combining the 1PEF imaging, 2PEF imaging, and FLIM results, we demonstrated that RCDs are a promising two-photon imaging agent. Our results indicate that the synthesized RCDs, exhibiting broadband NLO properties, show great potential for applications in the fields of saturable absorbers, bio-sensing, and imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 12004440, 61874141, and 61875232), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 2021JJ40704), and Postgraduate Innovative Project of Central South University (Grant No. 2021zzts0500).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rinp.2022.105591.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of carbon quantum dots and their applications in wastewater treatment
